---
title: " Bayesian inference in non-Gaussian time series with global-local
shrinkage process priors"
author: "Mattias Villani"
institute: "Department of Statistics, Stockholm University"

format:
  revealjs:
    # ── Theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
    css: ../custom.css
    include-after-body: 
      - ../cursor.js
      - ../logo.js

    # ── Math: SVG output is resolution-independent ─────────────────────────────
    html-math-method:
      method: mathjax
      url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg.js"

    # ── Resolution: more pixels → sharper rendering ────────────────────────────
    width:  1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05

    # ── Navigation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    slide-number:     "c/t"
    progress:         true
    history:          false
    hash:             true
    controls:         false       # cleaner without arrow buttons
    transition:       none        # instant transitions look more professional
    transition-speed: fast

    # ── Code ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    highlight-style:  github
    code-line-numbers: false
    
    #footer: "NordStat2026"

    # ── PDF export (via decktape or browser print) ──────────────────────────────
    # Run:  quarto render talk.qmd --to revealjs
    # PDF:  open in browser → Print → Save as PDF (set margins to None)
    
resources:
  - figs/

# ── Execution ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
execute:
  echo:    true
  warning: false
  eval:    false

# ── Julia engine ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
jupyter: julia-1.12

# ── Bibliography ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# bibliography: references.bib
---

## Outline

1. Dynamic shrinkage process priors
2. Time-varying multi-seasonal ARMA
3. Non-Gaussian models 
4. Posterior inference

---


## The parametric bottleneck

This is [important]{.alertgold} and this is [a definition]{.alertblue}.

$$p(y \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f(y \mid \mu_k, \sigma_k^2)$$

**Problems:**

- $K$ must be fixed before seeing data
- Model selection over $K$ is awkward
- True $K$ may be unknown or infinite

::: {.highlight-box}
**Idea:** put a prior directly over the **space of distributions** — let the data determine complexity.
:::

---

## The Dirichlet Process {.theorem-slide}

::: {.callout-note .callout-theorem appearance="simple"}
**Definition (Ferguson 1973).** A random probability measure $G$ on $(\Theta, \mathcal{B})$ is a **Dirichlet Process** with base measure $G_0$ and concentration $\alpha > 0$, written

$$G \sim \mathrm{DP}(\alpha, G_0),$$

if for every finite measurable partition $\{B_1, \ldots, B_k\}$ of $\Theta$,

$$(G(B_1), \ldots, G(B_k)) \sim \mathrm{Dir}(\alpha G_0(B_1), \ldots, \alpha G_0(B_k)).$$
:::

**Key properties:**

- $\mathbb{E}[G(B)] = G_0(B)$ — the base measure is the prior mean
- $\mathrm{Var}[G(B)] = \frac{G_0(B)(1-G_0(B))}{\alpha + 1}$ — $\alpha$ controls concentration
- Realizations are [discrete]{.alertblue} with probability one

---

# Stick-breaking representation

::: {.cols-6-4}
::: {}
Sethuraman (1994): $G \sim \mathrm{DP}(\alpha, G_0)$ if and only if

$$G = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} w_k \, \delta_{\theta_k}$$

where $\theta_k \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} G_0$ and

$$w_k = v_k \prod_{j < k} (1 - v_j), \quad v_k \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{Beta}(1, \alpha).$$

The weights $(w_k)$ satisfy $\sum_{k=1}^\infty w_k = 1$ a.s.

**Interpretation of $\alpha$:** small $\alpha \Rightarrow$ few dominant atoms; large $\alpha \Rightarrow$ many small atoms, $G \approx G_0$.
:::

::: {}
```{julia}
#| label: fig-stickbreaking
#| fig-cap: "Stick-breaking weights for two values of α"
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
using Distributions, CairoMakie

function stickbreak(α, K=30)
    v = rand(Beta(1, α), K)
    w = similar(v)
    rem = 1.0
    for k in 1:K
        w[k] = v[k] * rem
        rem  *= (1 - v[k])
    end
    w
end

fig = Figure(size=(500, 320))
for (i, α) in enumerate([1.0, 10.0])
    ax = Axis(fig[1, i],
        title  = "α = $α",
        xlabel = "Atom index k",
        ylabel = i==1 ? "Weight wₖ" : "")
    ws = stickbreak(α)
    barplot!(ax, 1:length(ws), ws, color=:steelblue)
end
fig
```
:::
:::

---

## Chinese Restaurant Process

The DP induces a **clustering distribution** on observations. If $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{n-1} \sim G$ have been drawn, then:

$$\theta_n \mid \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{n-1} \sim \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + n - 1} G_0 + \frac{1}{\alpha + n - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \delta_{\theta_i}$$

This is the **Pólya urn scheme**. Cluster structure: customer $n$ joins

| Event | Probability |
|---|---|
| Table $k$ (already occupied by $n_k$ customers) | $n_k / (\alpha + n - 1)$ |
| New table | $\alpha / (\alpha + n - 1)$ |

Expected number of clusters: $\mathbb{E}[K_n] \approx \alpha \log n$.

---

## DP Mixture Model

Combine the DP prior with a likelihood to get a **flexible density estimator**:

$$\begin{aligned}
G &\sim \mathrm{DP}(\alpha, G_0) \\
\phi_i \mid G &\overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} G \\
y_i \mid \phi_i &\sim f(y \mid \phi_i)
\end{aligned}$$

Marginalizing over $G$ gives a random partition model. With $G_0 = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \kappa_0^{-1})$ and $f(y \mid \phi) = \mathcal{N}(y \mid \phi, \sigma^2)$ this is a **Gaussian DP mixture**.

::: {.highlight-box}
The posterior $G \mid y_1, \ldots, y_n$ is again a DP:

$$G \mid y_{1:n} \sim \mathrm{DP}\!\left(\alpha + n,\; \frac{\alpha}{\alpha+n} G_0 + \frac{1}{\alpha+n}\sum_{i=1}^n \delta_{y_i}\right)$$
:::

---

## Gibbs Sampler for DP Mixtures

Collapsed sampler (Neal 2000, Algorithm 3). Let $z_i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ be the cluster label for observation $i$.

For each $i = 1, \ldots, n$:

1. Remove $i$ from its cluster; update counts $n_{-i,k}$
2. Sample new label from:

$$p(z_i = k \mid z_{-i}, y) \propto \begin{cases}
n_{-i,k} \cdot p(y_i \mid y_{-i, k}) & k \text{ existing} \\[4pt]
\alpha \cdot p(y_i \mid G_0) & k \text{ new}
\end{cases}$$

where $p(y_i \mid y_{-i,k})$ is the posterior predictive under $G_0$.

For conjugate $G_0$ this is available in **closed form** — no MH step needed.

---

## Implementation in Julia

```{julia}
#| label: lst-gibbs
#| eval: false

"""One full Gibbs sweep for a Gaussian DP mixture (conjugate NIG prior)."""
function gibbs_sweep!(z, y, α, μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀)
    n = length(y)
    for i in 1:n
        z[i] = 0                          # remove observation i
        k_vals, log_probs = Int[], Float64[]

        for k in unique(z[z .> 0])
            idx   = findall(==(k), z)
            nk    = length(idx)
            log_p = log(nk) + log_pred(y[i], y[idx], μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀)
            push!(k_vals, k); push!(log_probs, log_p)
        end

        # new cluster
        push!(k_vals, maximum(z) + 1)
        push!(log_probs, log(α) + log_pred(y[i], Float64[], μ₀, κ₀, a₀, b₀))

        probs  = softmax(log_probs)
        z[i]   = k_vals[rand(Categorical(probs))]
    end
    return z
end
```

---

## Dynamic shrinkage process priors - local level model

::: {.iframe-slide}
<iframe width="100%" height="807" frameborder="0"
  src="https://observablehq.com/embed/@mattiasvillani/local-level-model-dsp-prior?cells=viewof+input%2Cviewof+plotselector%2Cviewof+simulatebutton%2Ctimeplot&banner=false"></iframe>
:::

---

## Summary

::: {.cols-2}
:::{}
**What we covered:**

- DP is a prior over distributions with two parameters: $\alpha$ (concentration) and $G_0$ (base measure)
- Stick-breaking gives a constructive representation
- CRP describes the induced clustering
- DP mixtures are flexible density estimators
- Conjugate Gibbs sampler is clean and efficient
:::
:::{}
**Key formulas:**

| Object | Expression |
|---|---|
| Prior mean | $\mathbb{E}[G] = G_0$ |
| CRP new table | $\alpha/(\alpha+n-1)$ |
| Posterior | $\mathrm{DP}(\alpha+n, \tilde{G})$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[K_n]$ | $\approx \alpha \log n$ |

:::
:::

---

## References {.smaller}

- Ferguson, T.S. (1973). A Bayesian analysis of some nonparametric problems. *Annals of Statistics*, 1(2), 209–230.
- Sethuraman, J. (1994). A constructive definition of Dirichlet priors. *Statistica Sinica*, 4, 639–650.
- Neal, R.M. (2000). Markov chain sampling methods for Dirichlet process mixture models. *JCGS*, 9(2), 249–265.
